convenience.label: Convenience
convenience.definition: Minimise friction, cognitive load and physical effort across the user journey.
convenience.lectureSummary: Design choices that make actions effortless and intuitive increase user speed and completion.

subPrinciples.label: Shortest Path to Conversion
subPrinciples.definition: Eliminate steps between intent and order completion.

subPrinciples.label: Smart Defaults & Pre-Fills
subPrinciples.definition: Anticipate user input using stored, context-aware or statistical defaults.

subPrinciples.label: Resume, Don't Restart
subPrinciples.definition: Preserve session state so users never repeat work.

subPrinciples.label: Progressive Disclosure
subPrinciples.definition: Show essential information first and reveal details on demand.

subPrinciples.label: Choice Reduction
subPrinciples.definition: Limit options at decision points to reduce cognitive fatigue.

subPrinciples.label: Clear Action Hierarchy
subPrinciples.definition: Direct visual focus toward the single most important action.

ethicalGuardrails: Convenience should simplify legitimate choices, not hide critical terms.
Never default to optional recurring charges or add-ons without clear consent.
Always allow users to easily review and edit auto-filled information.

loss-avoidance.label: Loss Avoidance
loss-avoidance.definition: Frame decisions around protecting value, progress or exclusive privileges.
loss-avoidance.lectureSummary: Users feel the pain of losing something more strongly than the pleasure of gaining an equal reward.

subPrinciples.label: Time Scarcity
subPrinciples.definition: Highlight time limits on availability or special conditions.

subPrinciples.label: Quantity Scarcity
subPrinciples.definition: Show low stock levels to signal high demand and impending loss.

subPrinciples.label: Progress Preservation
subPrinciples.definition: Protect effort, earned status or discounts already accumulated.

subPrinciples.label: Privilege Preservation
subPrinciples.definition: Remind users of earned member rights that expire if unacted upon.

subPrinciples.label: Reference Dependence
subPrinciples.definition: Anchor value against higher original prices or lost savings.

ethicalGuardrails: Timers and inventory counters must reflect real backend conditions.
Do not generate artificial urgency or fake stock warnings.
Clearly distinguish between temporary holds and actual commitments.

status.label: Status
status.definition: Make rank, progression, exclusivity and earned privileges visible and meaningful.
status.lectureSummary: Users are motivated by recognised standing, advancement and access to differentiated benefits.

subPrinciples.label: Creating Desire Through Aspirational Marketing
subPrinciples.definition: Make the product, membership or higher tier feel desirable before asking the user to pursue it.

subPrinciples.label: Inspiring Aspiration Through Status
subPrinciples.definition: Show users what higher status looks like and how their future experience could improve.
featureExamples: Showing the lifestyle or privileges associated with higher tiers
Presenting the next tier as achievable
Comparing current and future benefits positively

subPrinciples.label: Pre-Ownership
subPrinciples.definition: Create emotional investment and insider identity before the user fully owns the product, service or status.
featureExamples: Adding premium items to a wishlist

subPrinciples.label: Establishing Status Differences
subPrinciples.definition: Make the difference between membership levels, achievements or access rights visible and understandable.
featureExamples: Elite Silver, Elite Gold and Elite Platinum badges

subPrinciples.label: Reward and Achievement Structures
subPrinciples.definition: Connect continued behaviour to incremental progress, milestones and rewards.
featureExamples: Remaining spend to the next tier

subPrinciples.label: Payment Versus Achievement
subPrinciples.definition: Allow users to attain or accelerate status through spending, payment or achievement, while keeping the distinction transparent and fair.
featureExamples: Spend $200 to receive double Elite credits
Spending thresholds that contribute to tier status
ethicalConsiderations: Clearly explain what can be earned, what can be purchased, and what can be accelerated.

subPrinciples.label: Encouraging Public Status Displays
subPrinciples.definition: Allow users to visibly display their rank, achievements or exclusive access.
featureExamples: Elite badge beside the user's name
VIP luggage tag or digital emblem

subPrinciples.label: Making Customers Feel Important
Recognise and elevate customers so they feel valued by the platform.
Ethical Status Design
Keep status aspirational, attainable and constructive.
patterns: Emphasise positive aspiration rather than humiliation
Give newcomers a visible path forward
Avoid portraying non-members or lower tiers as inferior
Preserve fairness between users who earn status and users who pay for it
Explain tier requirements and benefits clearly
Avoid encouraging unaffordable or harmful spending
featureExamples: Fairness between earned and paid status

ethicalGuardrails: Emphasise positive aspiration rather than humiliation or devaluation.
Provide realistic incremental goals and give newcomers a visible path forward.
Avoid portraying non-members or lower tiers as inferior.
Preserve fairness between users who earn status and users who pay for or accelerate it.
Explain tier requirements, multipliers and benefits clearly, avoiding encouraging unaffordable or harmful spending.
